chunk_summary: This section provides phenotype-specific dietary strategies for Indian children with dyslipidemia and MASLD, focusing on reducing hepatic lipogenesis, managing lipid profiles, and aligning diets with glycemic and lipid goals.
learning_objectives: Understand dietary modifications for high TG/low HDL-C and high LDL-C phenotypes in children.
Learn practical strategies to reduce hepatic de novo lipogenesis and improve lipid profiles.
Identify culturally appropriate dietary substitutions for Indian families managing MASLD.
Recognize the importance of rechecking fasting lipids and monitoring ALT trends.
key_facts: For high TG/low HDL-C: Reduce free sugars and refined starch; recheck fasting lipids in 8–12 weeks.
For high LDL-C: Lower saturated fat, trans-fat, and dietary cholesterol; increase soluble fiber intake.
MASLD management: Avoid sugar-sweetened beverages, control refined starch portions, and promote daily activity.
Protein is required with every meal; examples include eggs, curd, paneer, pulses, and soy.
Omega-3 sources like fish, walnuts, and flaxseed are recommended for MASLD where culturally acceptable.
Counsel families that 'fruit juice is sugar for the liver' to emphasize sugar elimination.
reference_quote: For suspected MASLD, align the diet with both lipid and glycemic goals: strict avoidance of sugar-sweetened beverages, portion control of refined starch, and a daily activity plan. (Source: ISPGHAN/Indian Pediatrics, 2024)
document_key_topics: Culturally appropriate dietary modifications for Indian families
Monitoring and reinforcing adherence to dietary changes
